sections.heading: Priority Queue
sections.body: Uniform Cost Search uses a priority queue for its frontier, always expanding the node with the lowest cumulative path cost so far — rather than a queue (BFS) or a stack (DFS), it's specifically ordered by cost.

sections.heading: Path Cost
sections.body: Path cost is the sum of the costs of every action taken to reach a given node from the initial state. UCS tracks this running total for every node in the frontier so it can always expand the cheapest one next.

sections.heading: Algorithm
sections.body: UCS repeatedly removes the lowest-cost node from the priority queue, checks if it's a goal, and if not, expands it by adding its children (with their updated cumulative costs) back into the priority queue — continuing until a goal node is dequeued.

sections.heading: Properties
sections.body: UCS is complete and optimal, even when step costs vary (unlike plain BFS, which is only optimal when all costs are equal) — as long as step costs are non-negative, so the cumulative cost never decreases as the path gets longer.

sections.heading: Applications
sections.body: UCS is well suited to problems like finding the cheapest route on a map where roads have different costs (distance, time, tolls) — it generalizes BFS's shortest-path idea to weighted, unequal-cost actions.

quiz.question: UCS always expands the node with:
options: The lowest cumulative path cost so far

quiz.question: How does UCS compare to BFS when step costs vary?
options: UCS remains optimal with varying costs; plain BFS is only optimal when all costs are equal
UCS cannot handle varying costs at all

quiz.question: UCS is guaranteed optimal as long as:
options: There is only one goal state
